choices.text: Resets browser default styles for form elements (inputs, selects, checkboxes, radios, etc.) so Tailwind utilities work consistently across browsers. Without this plugin, browser defaults override your utility styling unpredictably — Chrome shows different focus rings than Firefox shows different selects vs Safari. The plugin gives you a clean canvas to style with utilities like `rounded-md border-gray-300 focus:border-brand-500 focus:ring focus:ring-brand-500/20`. Essential for any app with forms. The plugin itself doesn't add validation logic — that's a separate concern (React Hook Form + Zod, covered in the React course).

questions.explanation: Resets browser form defaults so Tailwind utilities can style consistently. Essential for any forms.

questions.prompt: You want a `text-shadow-md` utility that doesn't exist in Tailwind. Right approach?

choices.text: Write a tiny custom plugin: `plugin(function({ addUtilities }) { addUtilities({ '.text-shadow-md': { textShadow: '0 2px 4px rgba(0,0,0,0.15)' }, ... }) })` in tailwind.config. Now `text-shadow-md` works as a utility with all Tailwind modifiers (`hover:text-shadow-lg`, `dark:text-shadow-none`). For values that should track your theme, use `theme()` inside the plugin to reference design tokens. Custom plugins are the right tool when you need a utility pattern across many components; inline styles lose composition with modifiers, and CSS classes lose tree-shaking and modifier support.

questions.explanation: Write a custom plugin with addUtilities. Gets full Tailwind composition (modifiers, tree-shaking).
